title: Geographic proximity dampens ideological policy disagreement in urban politics
data_sources: Borealis / Scholars Portal Dataverse replication archive
core_conclusion: Left-right ideology structures attitudes toward municipal policy, but its influence is significantly dampened when residents consider the same issues at the geographically proximate scale of their own neighborhood. A topic-sampling experiment (40 issues, >40,000 preferences, ~4,000 Canadian respondents) reconciles the view that urban politics is ideological with the view that it is place-based and non-ideological: both hold, depending on geographic scale.
why_it_matters: Reconciles a long-standing debate in urban scholarship by showing the answer depends on geographic scale; isolates proximity as a causal lever via randomized framing; generalizes across 40 issues through topic sampling; suggests neighborhood-scale deliberation can defuse ideological gridlock.
why_nature: Bridges urban politics, political psychology and geography around one testable mechanism; advances from 'ideology matters' to when and why it matters; methodologically rigorous topic-sampling design with tens of thousands of preferences; directly policy-translatable for municipal consultation.
added_value_to_my_research: Measured (not verbal) proximity via 2SFCA/R2SFCA access scores as attitude predictors
Coupling neighborhood segregation/sorting indices with individual outcomes via multi-source fusion
Experienced-segregation logic transfers to mobility-weighted exposure in R2SFCA
Treat ideology x proximity interaction as a spatially non-stationary MGWR + explainable-ML surface
Embed measured response curves in agent-based / network models of local deliberation
